title: amqplib connect with rejectUnauthorized: false — the RabbitMQ broker certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: amqplib connect is called with rejectUnauthorized: false in its socket options, disabling validation of the RabbitMQ broker's TLS certificate. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the connection — reading and injecting messages on every queue (jobs, events, credentials). Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; trust the broker CA via the `ca` socket option.

fixed.detail: The amqplib connection validates the broker certificate (rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the amqps:// connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: new ApolloServer with csrfPrevention: false — the GraphQL API accepts simple-request CSRF

vulnerable.detail: new ApolloServer is created with csrfPrevention: false, disabling the check that requires a non-simple (preflighted) request. A malicious page can now trigger state-changing GraphQL operations using the victim's cookies via a simple cross-site request (CSRF) — no token, no preflight. Leave csrfPrevention enabled (the Apollo Server 4 default).

fixed.detail: ApolloServer keeps CSRF prevention on (csrfPrevention is not false) — mutations require a preflighted request, blocking simple-request cross-site forgery.

title: S3 putObject with ACL: "public-read" — every uploaded object is world-readable

vulnerable.detail: putObject is called with ACL: "public-read", which makes the uploaded object readable by ANYONE on the internet who knows or guesses the URL. If user uploads (documents, avatars, exports) flow through here, they are silently exposed publicly. Uploads succeed and the app works, so it ships. Use ACL: "private" (the default) and serve objects through pre-signed URLs.

fixed.detail: The S3 put uses a non-public ACL (not "public-read") — the object is not world-readable.

title: AWS SDK v3 PutObjectCommand with ACL: "public-read" — every uploaded object is world-readable

vulnerable.detail: new PutObjectCommand is created with ACL: "public-read", making the uploaded object readable by ANYONE on the internet who knows or guesses the URL. If user uploads flow through this command they are silently exposed publicly. Uploads succeed and the app works, so it ships. Use ACL: "private" (the default) and serve objects via pre-signed URLs.

fixed.detail: The PutObjectCommand uses a non-public ACL (not "public-read") — the object is not world-readable.

title: addAmounts sums token amounts as Number — silently loses precision above 2^53

fixed.detail: Whether this code computes the correct output is decided by the differential oracle (golden I/O), not static analysis. Re-run with --oracle=differential (opt-in; executes the candidate in a sandbox).

title: new cassandra-driver Client with sslOptions.rejectUnauthorized: false — the Cassandra TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new cassandra-driver Client is created with sslOptions: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling validation of the Cassandra/ScyllaDB node's TLS certificate. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the connection — exposing every query, credential, and row. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; provide the cluster CA via sslOptions.ca.

fixed.detail: The Cassandra client validates the node certificate (sslOptions.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: cookie.serialize with httpOnly: false — the auth cookie is readable from JavaScript (XSS session theft)

vulnerable.detail: cookie.serialize is called with httpOnly: false, so the Set-Cookie it builds is readable by JavaScript via document.cookie. Any XSS on the origin can exfiltrate the session/auth token and hijack the account. Set httpOnly: true for authentication cookies.

fixed.detail: cookie.serialize sets httpOnly: true — the cookie is not exposed to document.cookie, so client-side scripts cannot read the token.

title: cookie-session with httpOnly: false — the session cookie is readable from JavaScript (XSS session theft)

vulnerable.detail: cookie-session is configured with httpOnly: false, exposing the signed session cookie to JavaScript via document.cookie. Any XSS on the origin can read and exfiltrate the session and hijack the user's account. Set httpOnly: true (the default) for session cookies.

fixed.detail: cookie-session sets httpOnly: true — the session cookie is not exposed to document.cookie and cannot be read by client-side scripts.

title: cookie-session with secure: false — the session cookie is sent over plain HTTP and can be sniffed

vulnerable.detail: cookie-session is configured with secure: false, so the session cookie is sent over plain HTTP. On any non-TLS hop an attacker can read the signed session and hijack the user's authenticated session. Set secure: true in production (behind `app.set('trust proxy', 1)` if terminating TLS at a proxy).

fixed.detail: cookie-session sets secure: true — the signed session cookie is only ever transmitted over HTTPS.

title: cors() with origin: true and credentials — reflects any caller's origin, leaking authenticated responses cross-site

vulnerable.detail: cors() is called with origin: true, which REFLECTS whatever Origin the request carries back in Access-Control-Allow-Origin. With credentials: true this defeats the browser's `*`-plus-credentials guard: every site the victim visits can make credentialed cross-origin calls and read the authenticated responses. Pin origin to an explicit allow-list (string, array, or a validating function), never `true`.

fixed.detail: cors() pins origin to an explicit trusted value (not the reflect-everything `true`) — cross-origin credentialed access is limited to that allow-list.

title: cors() configured with origin: "*" — any website can call the API with the user's session

vulnerable.detail: cors() is called with origin: "*", allowing ANY website to make cross-origin requests to this API. Combined with credentials, this lets a malicious page act as the logged-in user. Set origin to an explicit allow-list (a string, array, or function), not "*".

fixed.detail: cors() sets an explicit allow-list origin (not "*") — cross-origin access is restricted to trusted sites.

title: applyDiscounts adds two percentages instead of compounding them — orders are massively over-discounted

fixed.detail: Whether this code computes the correct output is decided by the differential oracle (golden I/O), not static analysis. Re-run with --oracle=differential (opt-in; executes the candidate in a sandbox).

title: new elasticsearch Client with tls.rejectUnauthorized: false — the search cluster certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new Client is created with tls: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling validation of the Elasticsearch cluster's TLS certificate. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the connection — reading indexed data (often PII/logs) and the API key or basic-auth credentials sent with each request. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; trust the cluster CA via tls.ca.

fixed.detail: The Elasticsearch client validates the cluster certificate (tls.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: express-fileupload with safeFileNames: false — uploaded filenames keep path/special characters (traversal)

vulnerable.detail: express-fileupload is configured with safeFileNames: false, so an uploaded file's name is used as-is — including `../`, path separators, and null bytes. If that name is passed to mv()/a filesystem path, an attacker can traverse out of the upload directory and overwrite arbitrary files (e.g. a config or a served script). Set safeFileNames: true (ideally with a regex) and never build a path from an untrusted filename.

fixed.detail: express-fileupload sanitizes uploaded filenames (safeFileNames is not false) — path separators and special characters are stripped before the name is used.

title: express-jwt with credentialsRequired: false — routes that look protected accept requests with NO token

vulnerable.detail: express-jwt is configured with credentialsRequired: false, so requests that carry NO Authorization token are allowed through instead of being rejected. Every route behind this middleware silently becomes optional-auth: an anonymous caller reaches the handler, and any code that assumes req.auth exists runs unauthenticated. Only set credentialsRequired: false on genuinely public routes; protected routes must leave it at the default (true).

fixed.detail: express-jwt requires credentials (credentialsRequired is not false) — a request without a valid token is rejected before it reaches the protected handler.

title: express-rate-limit with max: 0 — the limiter is disabled, so there is no brute-force / abuse throttle

vulnerable.detail: express-rate-limit is configured with max: 0, which disables the limit entirely (0 means no cap). The middleware is mounted and looks like it protects the route, but it never throttles anything — login, OTP, and password-reset endpoints can be brute-forced without limit. Set max to a real per-window cap (e.g. 5 for auth endpoints).

fixed.detail: express-rate-limit sets a positive max — requests beyond the threshold in the window are throttled.

title: express-session with cookie.sameSite: "none" — the session cookie is sent on cross-site requests (CSRF)

vulnerable.detail: express-session is configured with cookie: { sameSite: "none" }, so the session cookie is attached to cross-site requests. This removes the SameSite CSRF defense: a malicious page can trigger state-changing requests that carry the victim's session. Unless a true cross-site flow is required (and paired with CSRF tokens), use sameSite: "lax" or "strict".

fixed.detail: express-session sets cookie.sameSite to a restrictive value ("lax" or "strict", not "none") — the session cookie is not sent on cross-site requests, blocking CSRF.

title: express-session with cookie.secure: false — the session cookie is sent over plain HTTP and can be sniffed

vulnerable.detail: express-session is configured with cookie: { secure: false }, so the session cookie is sent over plain HTTP as well as HTTPS. On any non-TLS hop an attacker can read the session id and hijack the authenticated session. Set cookie.secure: true in production (with app.set('trust proxy', 1) when TLS terminates at a proxy).

fixed.detail: express-session sets cookie.secure to true — the session cookie is only ever transmitted over HTTPS.

title: express-session configured with saveUninitialized: true — empty, unauthenticated sessions are persisted (session fixation + resource exhaustion)

vulnerable.detail: express-session is configured with saveUninitialized: true, so a brand-new, never-modified (unauthenticated) session is forced into the store and a Set-Cookie is sent to every visitor. This enables session fixation — an attacker can pre-seed a session id and get it accepted — and lets anonymous traffic exhaust the session store. The express-session docs recommend false; set saveUninitialized: false.

fixed.detail: express-session sets saveUninitialized to false — a session cookie is only established once something is actually stored on the session (e.g. after login), preventing empty-session persistence.

title: @fastify/cors registered with origin: "*" — any website can call the API with the user's session

vulnerable.detail: @fastify/cors is registered with origin: "*", allowing ANY website to make cross-origin requests to this API. Combined with credentials this lets a malicious page act as the logged-in user. Set origin to an explicit allow-list (a string, array, or function), not "*".

fixed.detail: @fastify/cors is registered with an explicit allow-list origin (not "*") — cross-origin access is restricted to trusted sites.

title: tls.Config{InsecureSkipVerify: true} disables TLS certificate verification (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: tls.Config sets InsecureSkipVerify: true, which turns OFF certificate and host-name verification. Any machine on the path can present a self-signed cert and silently MITM the connection. Remove it (default false); for a private CA set RootCAs instead.

fixed.detail: tls.Config sets InsecureSkipVerify to a safe value — the server's certificate chain and host name are verified.

title: got() with https.rejectUnauthorized: false — the HTTP client skips TLS certificate validation (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: got is called with https: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling TLS certificate validation for the request. Any machine on the network path can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the call — capturing credentials, tokens, and response data. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; pass a trusted CA via https.certificateAuthority if a private CA is required.

fixed.detail: got keeps certificate validation on (https.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the server's TLS identity is verified.

title: helmet({ contentSecurityPolicy: false }) — Content-Security-Policy is turned off, removing the main XSS mitigation

vulnerable.detail: helmet is configured with contentSecurityPolicy: false, which DISABLES the Content-Security-Policy header entirely. CSP is the primary defense-in-depth against XSS and data injection; with it off, any injected script runs unrestricted and can exfiltrate tokens or data. Leave CSP enabled (helmet's default) and configure directives instead of turning it off.

fixed.detail: helmet keeps Content-Security-Policy on (contentSecurityPolicy is not false) — a CSP header is sent that constrains where scripts and resources can load from.

title: helmet({ frameguard: false }) — X-Frame-Options is off, exposing the app to clickjacking

vulnerable.detail: helmet is configured with frameguard: false, which removes the X-Frame-Options header. Any site can now embed this app in an invisible iframe and trick a logged-in user into clicking hidden controls (clickjacking) — approving actions they never intended. Leave frameguard enabled (helmet's default DENY/SAMEORIGIN).

fixed.detail: helmet keeps frameguard on (frameguard is not false) — an X-Frame-Options header is sent that stops the app being embedded in a hostile frame.

title: helmet({ hsts: false }) — Strict-Transport-Security is off, allowing HTTPS-downgrade attacks

vulnerable.detail: helmet is configured with hsts: false, removing the Strict-Transport-Security header entirely. Browsers are no longer told to use HTTPS-only, so a network attacker can strip TLS and serve the site over plain HTTP (SSL-strip), intercepting credentials and session cookies. Leave HSTS enabled (helmet's default) with a real max-age.

fixed.detail: helmet keeps HSTS on (hsts is not false) — a Strict-Transport-Security header pins browsers to HTTPS.

title: helmet.hsts with maxAge: 0 — HSTS is disabled, leaving users open to HTTPS-downgrade (SSL-strip) attacks

vulnerable.detail: helmet.hsts is configured with maxAge: 0, which tells browsers to IMMEDIATELY EXPIRE the Strict-Transport-Security policy — HSTS is effectively off. A network attacker can strip TLS and serve the site over plain HTTP (SSL-strip), intercepting credentials and session cookies. Set maxAge to a real duration (e.g. 15552000 seconds = 180 days).

fixed.detail: helmet.hsts sets a non-zero maxAge — browsers are told to use HTTPS for the site for that duration, preventing protocol downgrade.

title: helmet({ noSniff: false }) — X-Content-Type-Options is off, allowing MIME-sniffing attacks

vulnerable.detail: helmet is configured with noSniff: false, removing the X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header. Browsers may now MIME-sniff a response and execute a user-uploaded file (or a text/plain endpoint reflecting user input) as HTML/JavaScript — turning a benign-looking upload or API into stored XSS. Leave noSniff enabled (helmet's default).

fixed.detail: helmet keeps noSniff on (noSniff is not false) — the X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header stops browsers from MIME-sniffing responses.

title: new https.Agent with rejectUnauthorized: false — every request through this agent skips TLS validation (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new https.Agent is created with rejectUnauthorized: false, disabling TLS certificate validation for EVERY request routed through this agent. When the agent is attached to axios/got/node-fetch (a common way to 'fix' a cert error globally), the whole HTTP client silently trusts forged certificates and can be man-in-the-middled. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; pass a proper `ca` if a private CA is required.

fixed.detail: The https.Agent keeps certificate validation on (rejectUnauthorized is not false) — connections made through it verify the server's TLS identity.

title: https.request with rejectUnauthorized: false — TLS certificate validation disabled (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: https.request is called with rejectUnauthorized: false, which turns OFF TLS certificate validation. Any machine on the network path can present a self-signed certificate and silently man-in-the-middle the connection — credentials and data are exposed. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; if a private CA is needed, pass `ca` instead.

fixed.detail: https.request keeps certificate validation on (rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the server's TLS identity is verified.

title: new Redis with tls.rejectUnauthorized: false — the Redis TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new Redis is created with tls: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling validation of the Redis server's TLS certificate. Anyone on the network path can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the connection — reading and tampering with cached sessions, rate-limit counters, and queues. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; trust the provider's CA via tls.ca.

fixed.detail: The ioredis client validates the server certificate (tls.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the Redis connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: iron-session with cookieOptions.secure: false — the encrypted session cookie is sent over plain HTTP

vulnerable.detail: getIronSession is configured with cookieOptions: { secure: false }, so the sealed session cookie is sent over plain HTTP. On any non-TLS hop an attacker can capture the sealed cookie and replay it to impersonate the user until it expires. Set cookieOptions.secure: true in production.

fixed.detail: iron-session sets cookieOptions.secure to true — the encrypted session cookie is only ever transmitted over HTTPS.

title: Jito bundle sent with a tip of 0 — bundle is deprioritized and will not land

vulnerable.detail: sendBundle is called with tipLamports of 0 (or new BN(0)). A Jito bundle with no tip is deprioritized by the block engine and, under any competition, never lands — but the call still returns a bundle id, so the code proceeds as if the transactions submitted. Set a nonzero tip (and ideally scale it with priority/competition).

fixed.detail: sendBundle's options object does not set tipLamports as an inline literal; the tip cannot be confirmed statically. Verify it resolves to a nonzero value.

title: jose SignJWT.setProtectedHeader with alg: "none" — the JWT is issued unsigned and forgeable

vulnerable.detail: setProtectedHeader is called with alg: "none", so jose issues an UNSECURED JWT with no signature. Anyone can craft a token with alg: none and arbitrary claims; wherever "none" is accepted it authenticates — a complete auth bypass. Set alg to a real algorithm (e.g. "HS256", "RS256", "EdDSA").

fixed.detail: The protected header pins a real signing algorithm (not "none") — the issued JWT carries a verifiable signature.

title: Jupiter quote/swap call hardcodes slippageBps to 0

vulnerable.detail: quoteGet is called with slippageBps: 0, which disables slippage protection entirely. Any price movement between the quote and the on-chain swap (including a sandwich attack) executes the trade at whatever price results, with no minimum-output guarantee. Set slippageBps to a nonzero value (e.g. 50 = 0.5%) or use `dynamicSlippage` so the swap reverts if the price moves too far.

fixed.detail: quoteGet's slippageBps is a nonzero literal — the swap has explicit slippage protection.

title: isExpired multiplies a seconds-based exp by 1000 — expired tokens are treated as valid forever

fixed.detail: Whether this code computes the correct output is decided by the differential oracle (golden I/O), not static analysis. Re-run with --oracle=differential (opt-in; executes the candidate in a sandbox).

title: express-jwt passes ignoreNotBefore:true through to jwt.verify, silently accepting not-yet-valid tokens

vulnerable.detail: expressjwt() is configured with ignoreNotBefore:true. express-jwt spreads the whole options object into jwt.verify(token, key, options) (src/index.ts:175), and Params extends jwt.VerifyOptions, so this flag flows straight through and disables 'nbf' enforcement. Tokens minted with a future not-before time are accepted as valid — the happy path compiles and runs, but pre-activation / future-dated tokens are honored.

fixed.detail: expressjwt() options do not set ignoreNotBefore:true, so the underlying jwt.verify enforces the 'nbf' (not-before) claim and rejects tokens that are not yet valid.

title: Mongoose index option dropDups:true is silently ignored, so duplicate uniques are never dropped

vulnerable.detail: index() options set dropDups:true. This flag was removed in MongoDB 3.0 / Mongoose 3.x and is silently ignored: the schema compiles and the app runs, but existing duplicate documents are NOT dropped and the developer's assumption that duplicates were cleaned up is false, leaving duplicate rows that break the intended uniqueness guarantee.

fixed.detail: index() options do not set the removed dropDups flag; uniqueness relies on a genuine unique index that MongoDB enforces.

title: jwt.sign with algorithm: "none" — tokens are issued unsigned and can be forged by anyone

vulnerable.detail: jwt.sign is called with algorithm: "none", so the issued token has an EMPTY signature. Anyone can craft a token with alg: none and arbitrary claims and it will pass verification wherever "none" is accepted — a total authentication bypass. Sign with a real algorithm (e.g. "HS256" or "RS256") and never issue unsigned tokens.

fixed.detail: jwt.sign issues the token with a real signing algorithm (not "none") — the token carries a verifiable signature.

title: jwt.sign with allowInsecureKeySizes: true — signs tokens with a key below the RSA minimum, weakening every signature

vulnerable.detail: jwt.sign is called with allowInsecureKeySizes: true, which suppresses jsonwebtoken's built-in guard that rejects RSA keys shorter than 2048 bits. Tokens then get signed with an undersized (brute-forceable) key and the signatures they carry are weak — an attacker who factors the small key can forge valid tokens. Remove the flag (or set it false) so the minimum-key-size check protects every signature.

fixed.detail: jwt.sign does not force allowInsecureKeySizes: true — the library enforces its minimum key-size check, so tokens are signed with an adequately sized key.

title: jwt.verify accepts the "none" algorithm — signature verification can be bypassed

vulnerable.detail: jwt.verify is called with "none" in its algorithms allow-list. An attacker can forge a token with alg: none and no signature, and it will be accepted — full authentication bypass. Remove "none" from algorithms; pin to the real signing algorithm(s) (e.g. ["RS256"]).

fixed.detail: jwt.verify's algorithms allow-list does not include "none" — unsigned tokens are rejected.

title: jwt.verify called with ignoreExpiration: true — expired tokens are accepted as valid

vulnerable.detail: jwt.verify is called with ignoreExpiration: true, which disables the `exp` (expiry) check entirely. A leaked or stolen token is then valid forever — a session/credential that should have expired is silently accepted. Remove ignoreExpiration (it defaults to false) or set it to false.

fixed.detail: jwt.verify sets ignoreExpiration to a value other than true — the `exp` claim is enforced and expired tokens are rejected.

title: new Kafka with ssl.rejectUnauthorized: false — the broker TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new Kafka is created with ssl: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling validation of the broker's TLS certificate. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the connection — reading and injecting messages on every topic (events, commands, PII streams). Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; trust the broker CA via ssl.ca.

fixed.detail: The Kafka client validates the broker certificate (ssl.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the broker connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: knex with connection.ssl.rejectUnauthorized: false — the database TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: knex is initialized with connection: { ssl: { rejectUnauthorized: false } }, disabling validation of the database server's TLS certificate for the whole pool. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the database — exposing every query, credential, and row. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; provide the provider's CA via connection.ssl.ca.

fixed.detail: knex validates the database server certificate (connection.ssl.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: koa-session with secure: false — the session cookie is sent over plain HTTP and can be sniffed

vulnerable.detail: koa-session is configured with secure: false, so the session cookie is sent over plain HTTP. On any non-TLS hop an attacker can read the session and hijack the authenticated session. Set secure: true in production (with app.proxy = true when TLS terminates at a proxy).

fixed.detail: koa-session sets secure: true — the session cookie is only ever transmitted over HTTPS.

title: ldapjs createClient with tlsOptions.rejectUnauthorized: false — the LDAP directory certificate is not validated (auth MITM)

vulnerable.detail: ldapjs createClient is configured with tlsOptions: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling validation of the LDAP directory server's certificate. Since LDAP bind carries user credentials, a network attacker who presents a forged certificate can man-in-the-middle the connection and harvest every username and password that authenticates through it. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; trust the directory's CA via tlsOptions.ca.

fixed.detail: The ldapjs client validates the directory server certificate (tlsOptions.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the LDAPS connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: libxmljs parseXml with noent: true — external entities are expanded, enabling XXE

vulnerable.detail: parseXml is called with noent: true, which turns ON entity substitution. A document with a DOCTYPE that declares an external entity (e.g. SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd" or an http:// URL) will have it EXPANDED during parsing — a classic XML External Entity (XXE) attack that leaks local files and enables SSRF against internal services. Remove noent: true; never enable entity substitution on untrusted XML.

fixed.detail: parseXml leaves entity substitution off (noent is not true) — external/general entities are not expanded, so XXE cannot read files or reach internal hosts.

title: isMutable: false on Metaplex NFT metadata permanently locks it — a broken URI or trait can never be fixed

vulnerable.detail: isMutable: false permanently freezes the NFT/collection metadata on-chain: the update authority can never fix a broken metadata URI, wrong trait, or dead image link. The mint succeeds and the happy path runs — the asset is just silently locked forever.

fixed.detail: isMutable: true — metadata stays updatable, so a broken URI or trait can be corrected later.

title: NFT minted with sellerFeeBasisPoints: 0 — creators receive zero royalties on secondary sales

vulnerable.detail: create() is called with sellerFeeBasisPoints: 0, which bakes zero royalties into the NFT's on-chain metadata. Secondary marketplaces that honour on-chain royalties will pay creators nothing. Metaplex token-metadata is immutable after mint — this cannot be corrected without burning and reminting. Set sellerFeeBasisPoints to the intended basis points (e.g. 500 = 5%).

fixed.detail: create() sets sellerFeeBasisPoints to a nonzero literal — creators will receive royalties on secondary sales.

title: Meteora DLMM swap called with minOutAmount of 0 — no slippage floor

vulnerable.detail: swap() is called with minOutAmount of 0 (or new BN(0)), which removes the minimum-output floor. Any price movement between quote and on-chain execution — including a sandwich attack — fills the swap at whatever price results, with no revert protection. Compute minOutAmount from the quote and a slippage tolerance (e.g. quote.minOutAmount or quote * (1 - 0.005)).

fixed.detail: minOutAmount is a non-literal expression — cannot determine statically

title: wrapIndex uses raw % which returns a negative for negative inputs — out-of-bounds ring/array index

fixed.detail: Whether this code computes the correct output is decided by the differential oracle (golden I/O), not static analysis. Re-run with --oracle=differential (opt-in; executes the candidate in a sandbox).

title: new MongoClient with tlsAllowInvalidCertificates: true — the database TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new MongoClient is created with tlsAllowInvalidCertificates: true, which turns OFF validation of the MongoDB server's TLS certificate. Anyone on the network path can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the database connection — every query, credential, and record is exposed. Remove tlsAllowInvalidCertificates: true; provide the proper CA via tlsCAFile if a private CA is used.

fixed.detail: MongoClient validates the server certificate (tlsAllowInvalidCertificates is not true) — the database connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: mongoose.connect with tlsAllowInvalidCertificates: true — the database TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: mongoose.connect is called with tlsAllowInvalidCertificates: true, which turns OFF validation of the MongoDB server's TLS certificate. Anyone on the network path can present a forged certificate and silently man-in-the-middle the database connection — every query and credential is exposed. Remove tlsAllowInvalidCertificates: true; supply the proper CA via `tlsCAFile` if a private CA is used.

fixed.detail: mongoose.connect validates the database server certificate (tlsAllowInvalidCertificates is not true) — the connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: mqtt.connect with rejectUnauthorized: false — the MQTT broker certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: mqtt.connect is called with rejectUnauthorized: false, disabling validation of the broker's TLS certificate. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the connection — reading and injecting messages on every topic (device telemetry, commands, credentials). For an IoT fleet this can mean spoofed sensor data or attacker-issued device commands. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; trust the broker CA via the `ca` option.

fixed.detail: The MQTT client validates the broker certificate (rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the mqtts:// connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: mssql connect with options.trustServerCertificate: true — the SQL Server TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: mssql.connect is configured with options: { trustServerCertificate: true }, which makes the driver trust ANY certificate the SQL Server presents. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the connection — every query, credential, and record is exposed. Remove trustServerCertificate: true; install and trust the server's real CA instead.

fixed.detail: The mssql connection validates the server certificate (options.trustServerCertificate is not true) — the database connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: mysql2 createConnection with ssl.rejectUnauthorized: false — the MySQL TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: createConnection is configured with ssl: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling validation of the MySQL server's TLS certificate. Anyone on the network path can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the database connection — every query and credential is exposed. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; provide the provider's CA via ssl.ca.

fixed.detail: The mysql2 connection validates the server certificate (ssl.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the database connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: NATS connect with tls.rejectUnauthorized: false — the NATS server certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: NATS connect is configured with tls: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling validation of the NATS server's TLS certificate. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the connection — reading and injecting messages on every subject (events, commands, credentials). Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; trust the server CA via tls.ca.

fixed.detail: The NATS client validates the server certificate (tls.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: nodemailer createTransport with tls.rejectUnauthorized: false — the SMTP server certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: createTransport is configured with tls: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling validation of the SMTP server's TLS certificate. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the mail connection — capturing SMTP credentials and the contents of every email sent (password resets, invites, magic links). Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; trust a private CA via tls.ca if needed.

fixed.detail: The transport validates the SMTP server certificate (tls.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the mail connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: offsetFor multiplies a 1-indexed page directly by page size — the first page of records is skipped

fixed.detail: Whether this code computes the correct output is decided by the differential oracle (golden I/O), not static analysis. Re-run with --oracle=differential (opt-in; executes the candidate in a sandbox).

title: passport-jwt Strategy with ignoreExpiration: true — expired JWTs are accepted forever

vulnerable.detail: The passport-jwt Strategy is constructed with ignoreExpiration: true, so the exp claim is NOT checked and expired tokens are accepted indefinitely. A token leaked or logged months ago still authenticates; revocation-by-expiry no longer works. Remove ignoreExpiration: true (the default false enforces expiry).

fixed.detail: The passport-jwt Strategy enforces expiry (ignoreExpiration is not true) — a token past its exp claim is rejected.

title: splitEqually floors each share and drops the remainder — the split sums to less than the total

fixed.detail: Whether this code computes the correct output is decided by the differential oracle (golden I/O), not static analysis. Re-run with --oracle=differential (opt-in; executes the candidate in a sandbox).

title: new pg Pool with ssl.rejectUnauthorized: false — the Postgres TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new Pool is created with ssl: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling validation of the Postgres server's TLS certificate. Anyone on the network path can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the database connection — every query, credential, and row is exposed. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; provide the managed provider's CA via ssl.ca instead.

fixed.detail: The pg Pool validates the Postgres server certificate (ssl.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the database connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: playwright newContext with ignoreHTTPSErrors: true — the browser context accepts any TLS certificate (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: browser.newContext is called with ignoreHTTPSErrors: true, so every page in this Playwright context accepts ANY TLS certificate. In a server-side scraper, SSR renderer, or automated flow that handles authenticated content, a network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the session — injecting content or capturing credentials. Remove ignoreHTTPSErrors: true; trust a private CA explicitly if one is required.

fixed.detail: browser.newContext validates TLS certificates (ignoreHTTPSErrors is not true) — the context rejects forged or invalid certificates.

title: puppeteer.launch with ignoreHTTPSErrors: true — the headless browser accepts any TLS certificate (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: puppeteer.launch is called with ignoreHTTPSErrors: true, so the headless browser accepts ANY TLS certificate — self-signed, expired, or attacker-supplied. When this browser fetches authenticated pages or scrapes data server-side, anyone on the network path can man-in-the-middle it and feed forged content or capture credentials. Remove ignoreHTTPSErrors: true; if a private CA is needed, trust it explicitly.

fixed.detail: puppeteer.launch validates TLS certificates (ignoreHTTPSErrors is not true) — the headless browser rejects forged or invalid certificates.

title: Pyth price read with getPriceUnchecked() instead of getPriceNoOlderThan()

vulnerable.detail: Handler calls getPriceUnchecked(), which returns the most recent Pyth price REGARDLESS of how old it is. If the feed stops updating (publisher outage, congestion), the protocol prices swaps/liquidations against a stale value with no guard. Use getPriceNoOlderThan(maxAge) (e.g. 60s) and handle the undefined/stale case explicitly.

fixed.detail: Handler reads the Pyth price via getPriceNoOlderThan(maxAge), which returns no price when the feed is older than maxAge — the caller can refuse to trade on a stale oracle.

title: Python fee calc divides before multiplying — integer truncation zeroes the fee

vulnerable.snippet:
def platform_fee(amount_cents, bps):
    # VULNERABLE: integer-divides FIRST, so any amount < 10000 cents truncates to
    # 0 and the fee is silently zero. Looks plausible; loses revenue on every small
    # charge.
    return amount_cents // 10000 * bps

title: Raydium computeAmountOut called with slippage: 0 — no minimum output protection against sandwich attacks

vulnerable.detail: computeAmountOut is called with slippage: 0, which sets minAmountOut equal to amountOut with zero tolerance. Any price movement between compute and execution — including a sandwich attack — will cause the swap to execute at a worse price with no revert protection. Set slippage to a nonzero value (e.g. 0.5 = 0.5%) to enforce a minAmountOut floor.

fixed.detail: computeAmountOut is called with a nonzero slippage value — the swap has minimum output protection against sandwich attacks.

title: node-redis createClient with socket.rejectUnauthorized: false — the Redis TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: createClient is configured with socket: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling validation of the Redis server's TLS certificate. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the connection — reading and tampering with cached sessions, rate-limit counters, and queues. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; trust the provider's CA via socket.ca.

fixed.detail: The node-redis client validates the server certificate (socket.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the rediss:// connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: res.cookie with httpOnly: false — the auth cookie is readable from JavaScript and can be stolen via XSS

vulnerable.detail: res.cookie is called with httpOnly: false, which makes the cookie readable by JavaScript via document.cookie. Any XSS anywhere on the origin can exfiltrate the session/auth token and hijack the account. Set httpOnly: true for authentication cookies.

fixed.detail: res.cookie sets httpOnly: true — the cookie is not exposed to document.cookie, so client-side scripts cannot read the session token.

title: new Sequelize with dialectOptions.ssl.rejectUnauthorized: false — the database TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new Sequelize is created with dialectOptions: { ssl: { rejectUnauthorized: false } }, disabling validation of the database server's TLS certificate for every connection in the pool. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the database — exposing every query, credential, and row. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; provide the managed provider's CA via dialectOptions.ssl.ca.

fixed.detail: Sequelize validates the database server certificate (dialectOptions.ssl.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: minAmountOut divides basis points by 100 instead of 10000 — slippage guard over-deducts, accepting catastrophic fills

fixed.detail: Whether this code computes the correct output is decided by the differential oracle (golden I/O), not static analysis. Re-run with --oracle=differential (opt-in; executes the candidate in a sandbox).

title: socket.io Server with cors.origin: "*" — any website can open an authenticated socket to the API

vulnerable.detail: new socket.io Server is created with cors: { origin: "*" }, allowing ANY website to open a Socket.IO connection to this server. Because the handshake carries the user's cookies, a malicious page can establish an authenticated real-time channel and drive or read events as the logged-in user. Set cors.origin to an explicit allow-list, not "*".

fixed.detail: The socket.io Server pins cors.origin to an explicit allow-list (not "*") — only trusted front-ends can open a socket.

title: connection.sendRawTransaction called with skipPreflight: true skips simulation and silently lands failing transfers

vulnerable.detail: sendRawTransaction is called with skipPreflight: true, disabling the preflight simulation. A malformed or failing transfer is submitted anyway; the happy path returns a signature but the transaction can fail or do the wrong thing on-chain with no client-side error surfaced to the user.

fixed.detail: sendRawTransaction runs preflight simulation (skipPreflight not set to true), so a transaction that would fail on-chain is rejected client-side before submission.

title: sendAndConfirmTransaction with commitment: "processed" — a transaction that can still be rolled back is treated as final

vulnerable.detail: sendAndConfirmTransaction is called with commitment: "processed", the weakest level: the transaction has merely been processed by the current leader and can still be dropped or rolled back if that fork does not win. Code that unlocks a purchase, credits a balance, or ships goods on this 'confirmation' acts on a state that may never finalize — a double-spend / phantom-payment window. Use "confirmed" or "finalized" before treating a transaction as settled.

fixed.detail: Confirmation waits for a durable commitment (not "processed") — the transaction is only treated as done once it is confirmed/finalized and cannot be dropped.

title: wallet.sendTransaction called with skipPreflight: true skips simulation and silently lands failing/wrong txs

vulnerable.detail: sendTransaction is called with skipPreflight: true, disabling the preflight simulation. A malformed or failing transaction is submitted anyway; the happy path returns a signature but the transaction can fail or do the wrong thing on-chain with no client-side error.

fixed.detail: sendTransaction runs preflight simulation (skipPreflight not set to true), so a transaction that would fail on-chain is rejected client-side before submission.

title: SOL→lamports conversion uses the wrong scaling constant (off by 1000x)

fixed.detail: Whether this code computes the correct output is decided by the differential oracle (golden I/O), not static analysis. Re-run with --oracle=differential (opt-in; executes the candidate in a sandbox).

title: sendAndConfirmTransaction (kit factory) called with skipPreflight: true skips simulation on a transfer

vulnerable.detail: sendAndConfirmTransaction is called with skipPreflight: true, disabling preflight simulation. A failing transfer is submitted anyway; the happy path returns without error but the tx can fail on-chain with no client-side signal.

fixed.detail: sendAndConfirmTransaction runs preflight simulation (skipPreflight not set to true), so a transfer that would fail on-chain is rejected client-side before submission.

title: sendRawTransaction with skipPreflight: true — a failing transaction is broadcast with no simulation, so errors surface silently (or never)

vulnerable.detail: sendRawTransaction is called with skipPreflight: true, so the client broadcasts the transaction WITHOUT the preflight simulation that normally catches a failing instruction. A transaction that reverts on-chain is sent anyway; the caller gets a signature and assumes success, while the intended state change (a transfer, a swap, a mint) silently never happened. Remove skipPreflight: true unless you independently simulate and confirm the transaction.

fixed.detail: sendRawTransaction runs preflight simulation (skipPreflight is not true) — a transaction that would fail is caught before it is broadcast and its error is surfaced to the caller.

title: sendTransaction used instead of sendAndConfirmTransaction — transaction may silently drop

vulnerable.detail: Handler calls connection.sendTransaction() but not sendAndConfirmTransaction(). sendTransaction() submits the transaction and returns a signature immediately, with no confirmation that it landed. If the transaction is dropped (congestion, validator restart, blockhash expiry) the code continues as if it succeeded — tokens are not moved but the caller assumes they were. Use sendAndConfirmTransaction(connection, tx, signers) to block until confirmation.

fixed.detail: Handler calls sendAndConfirmTransaction, which waits for the cluster to confirm the transaction before returning and propagates any on-chain error as a thrown exception.

title: Authorization via tx.origin — a relaying contract passes the owner check (phishing)

vulnerable.detail: Authorization uses tx.origin. tx.origin is the ORIGINAL external account, so if the owner is tricked into calling a malicious contract, that contract can relay the call and this check passes. Use msg.sender (the immediate caller) for authorization.

fixed.detail: Authorization does not rely on tx.origin — msg.sender (the immediate caller) is the correct identity to check.

title: Token payout uses createTransferInstruction instead of createTransferCheckedInstruction

vulnerable.detail: Payout builds its transfer with createTransferInstruction, the legacy SPL Token instruction. It performs no on-chain validation that the source and destination accounts belong to the expected mint or that the amount is scaled to the correct decimals. If a caller (or a chain of account-derivation bugs) supplies a token account for the wrong mint, the transfer can move the wrong asset or move the right asset at the wrong decimal scale, with no protocol-level check to stop it. Replace with createTransferCheckedInstruction(source, mint, destination, owner, amount, decimals).

fixed.detail: Payout uses createTransferCheckedInstruction, which validates the mint and decimals of the source/destination token accounts before building the transfer.

title: oidcProvider({ allowPlainCodeChallengeMethod: true }) — accepts 'plain' PKCE, which provides no protection against code interception

vulnerable.detail: allowPlainCodeChallengeMethod: true lets clients use code_challenge_method='plain', where the code_challenge equals the raw code_verifier. An attacker who can read the authorization request (the exact threat PKCE exists to defeat) sees the plain verifier and can redeem an intercepted authorization code — plain PKCE gives zero protection. RFC 7636 recommends S256; OAuth 2.1 disallows plain. Fix: remove this option or set allowPlainCodeChallengeMethod: false.

fixed.detail: allowPlainCodeChallengeMethod is false, so only the S256 code_challenge_method is accepted. S256 sends only a SHA-256 hash of the verifier in the authorization request, so intercepting the request does not reveal the verifier.

title: oidcProvider({ requirePKCE: false }) — OIDC provider issues authorization codes without PKCE, enabling code-interception attacks

vulnerable.detail: requirePKCE: false disables PKCE enforcement — the provider will happily issue authorization codes to clients that send no code_challenge. An attacker who intercepts an authorization code (via a malicious app registered on a redirect scheme, referer leakage, or a compromised network) can redeem it directly, because there is no code_verifier binding the code to the original client. OAuth 2.1 REQUIRES PKCE. Fix: remove this option or set requirePKCE: true.

fixed.detail: requirePKCE is true (the default), so the OIDC provider rejects any authorization request lacking a code_challenge with 'pkce is required'. This is what OAuth 2.1 mandates for all authorization-code flows.

title: Stripe PaymentIntent with application_fee_amount: 0 — the platform silently collects no fee on connected-account charges

vulnerable.detail: The PaymentIntent is created with application_fee_amount: 0 on a connected-account (Stripe Connect) charge. The payment succeeds and the customer is charged in full, but the platform's fee is ZERO — 100% of every transaction goes to the connected account and the marketplace silently earns nothing. This looks correct in every test (payments work) and is only caught when revenue is reconciled. Compute application_fee_amount from the order total (e.g. a platform percentage), never a hardcoded 0.

fixed.detail: The PaymentIntent sets a non-zero application_fee_amount — the platform collects its fee on each connected-account charge.

title: Stripe payment call targets a resource that does not exist at the pinned version

fixed.detail: Whether this code type-checks against stripe@17.7.0 is decided by the compiler oracle, not static analysis. Re-run with --oracle=compiler (or --oracle=best).

title: new tedious Connection with options.trustServerCertificate: true — the SQL Server TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new tedious Connection is created with options: { trustServerCertificate: true }, which makes the driver trust ANY certificate the SQL Server presents. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the connection — exposing every query, credential, and record. Remove trustServerCertificate: true; install and trust the server's real CA instead.

fixed.detail: The tedious Connection validates the server certificate (options.trustServerCertificate is not true) — the SQL Server connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: toBaseUnits scales by 10^(decimals-1) — every amount is 10x too small

fixed.detail: Whether this code computes the correct output is decided by the differential oracle (golden I/O), not static analysis. Re-run with --oracle=differential (opt-in; executes the candidate in a sandbox).

title: new TypeORM DataSource with ssl.rejectUnauthorized: false — the database TLS certificate is not validated (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new DataSource is created with ssl: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling validation of the database server's TLS certificate for every connection in the pool. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the database — exposing every query, credential, and row. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; provide the provider's CA via ssl.ca.

fixed.detail: The TypeORM DataSource validates the database server certificate (ssl.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the connection's TLS identity is verified.

title: new undici Agent with connect.rejectUnauthorized: false — the HTTP client skips TLS certificate validation (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new undici Agent is created with connect: { rejectUnauthorized: false }, disabling TLS certificate validation for every request routed through it. When set as the global dispatcher (a common way to 'fix' cert errors for fetch/undici), the whole HTTP client silently trusts forged certificates and can be man-in-the-middled. Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; pass a trusted CA via connect.ca if a private CA is required.

fixed.detail: The undici Agent validates server certificates (connect.rejectUnauthorized is not false) — TLS identity is verified for every request it dispatches.

title: sendTransaction result used without waitForTransactionReceipt — the hash is returned as if the transaction were mined

vulnerable.detail: sendTransaction returns a transaction HASH the moment the tx is broadcast — it is NOT a confirmation that the transaction was mined or that it succeeded. This scope never calls waitForTransactionReceipt, so the hash is handed back as if the transfer had settled. If the tx is dropped from the mempool, reverts, or is replaced, downstream logic (crediting a balance, marking an order paid, releasing goods) proceeds on a transaction that never landed. Await publicClient.waitForTransactionReceipt({ hash }) and check receipt.status === "success" before treating the send as final.

fixed.detail: sendTransaction is followed by waitForTransactionReceipt in the same scope — the code blocks until the transaction is mined and can act on the receipt (status, revert, logs) instead of a bare hash.

title: new WebSocket with rejectUnauthorized: false — the wss:// connection skips TLS certificate validation (MITM)

vulnerable.detail: new WebSocket is created with rejectUnauthorized: false, disabling TLS certificate validation on the wss:// connection. A network attacker can present a forged certificate and man-in-the-middle the real-time channel — reading and injecting messages (auth tokens, trades, commands). Remove rejectUnauthorized: false; pass a proper `ca` if a private CA is required.

fixed.detail: The WebSocket keeps certificate validation on (rejectUnauthorized is not false) — the wss:// server's TLS identity is verified.
